docs.title: Kubernet deployment platfrom docs
docs.text: Welcome to the Kubernet deployment platform.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Quick start guide
  • Cluster setup
  • Helm charts
  • CICD pipelines
  • Auto scaling
  • Monitoring
  • Rollbacks
  • Secrets management

docs.title: Developer Quick Start Guide
docs.text:
Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases. Please report issues to your platform team.

Welcome to the Kubernetes Deployment Platform. This guide gets you from zero to a running microservice in under 30 minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin, make sure you have the following installed on your local machine:

  • kubectl v1.28+ — Install guide
  • Helm v3.12+ — Install guide
  • Docker v24+ (for building images)
  • Platform CLI (kdp-cli) — install via brew install kdp-cli or download from the internal portal
  • Access credentials to the container registry (request from your platform admin)

docs.title: Step 1 — Authenticate
docs.text:
Log in to the platform and configure your local kubeconfig:

kdp-cli login
kdp-cli cluster use <your-team-cluster>
kubectl get nodes   # verify connectivity

docs.title: Step 2 — Create Your Namespace
docs.text:
Each team works in an isolated namespace. Create one for your project:

kubectl create namespace my-service
kubectl label namespace my-service team=my-team env=dev

docs.title: Step 3 — Build and Push Your Container Image
docs.text:
docker build -t registry.internal.example.com/my-team/my-service:0.1.0 .
docker push registry.internal.example.com/my-team/my-service:0.1.0

docs.title: Step 4 — Deploy with Helm
docs.text:
Use the platform's base Helm chart to deploy your service:

helm repo add kdp https://charts.internal.example.com
helm repo update

helm install my-service kdp/microservice \
  --namespace my-service \
  --set image.repository=registry.internal.example.com/my-team/my-service \
  --set image.tag=0.1.0 \
  --set service.port=8080

docs.title: Step 5 — Verify the Deployment
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n my-service
kubectl get svc -n my-service
kubectl logs -n my-service deploy/my-service --tail=50

Your service is live once all pods show Running status.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Topic File Cluster Setup 02-cluster-setup.md Helm Charts 03-helm-charts.md CI/CD Pipelines 04-cicd-pipelines.md Autoscaling 05-autoscaling.md Monitoring 06-monitoring.md Rollbacks 07-rollbacks.md Secrets Management 08-secrets-management.md

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: Cluster Setup
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The platform manages one or more Kubernetes clusters provisioned on your cloud provider. Each cluster is segmented by environment (development, staging, production) and isolated using namespaces and RBAC policies.

docs.title: Cluster Tiers
docs.text: Tier Purpose Node Size Autoscaling dev Development & testing 2–4 nodes, 4 vCPU / 16 GB Off staging Pre-production validation 3–8 nodes, 8 vCPU / 32 GB On prod Live production workloads 5–20 nodes, 16 vCPU / 64 GB On

docs.title: Requesting a Cluster
docs.text:
Clusters are provisioned by the platform team. Submit a request through the internal portal with:

  • Team name and cost centre
  • Target cloud region
  • Expected workload type (stateless API, stateful database sidecar, batch job, etc.)
  • Required tier

Provisioning typically takes 15–30 minutes.

docs.title: Connecting to a Cluster
docs.text:
Once provisioned, credentials are distributed via the platform CLI:

kdp-cli cluster list               # list clusters you have access to
kdp-cli cluster use prod-us-east-1 # merge kubeconfig entry
kubectl cluster-info               # confirm connection

docs.title: Namespace Conventions
docs.text:
Teams are allocated one namespace per cluster tier:

<team-name>-dev
<team-name>-staging
<team-name>-prod

Resource quotas are applied at the namespace level. To view your current quota:

kubectl describe resourcequota -n my-team-prod

docs.title: Node Pools
docs.text:
The platform supports multiple node pools optimised for different workload types. Specify the pool via a node selector in your deployment manifest:

spec:
  nodeSelector:
    kdp.io/pool: compute   # options: general, compute, memory, gpu

docs.title: RBAC and Access Control
docs.text:
Access is role-based. Default roles are:

Role Permissions viewer Read-only access to namespace resources developer Deploy, scale, and read logs within the namespace admin Full namespace control, including secrets

Request role changes through the internal access management portal. The platform team does not grant cluster-level admin to individual users in production.

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: Helm Charts
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The platform provides a curated Helm chart library at https://charts.internal.example.com. These charts encapsulate best-practice Kubernetes resource configurations for common microservice patterns, so teams can deploy without writing raw YAML.

docs.title: Adding the Chart Repository
docs.text:
helm repo add kdp https://charts.internal.example.com
helm repo update

docs.title: Available Charts
docs.text: Chart Description kdp/microservice Stateless HTTP/gRPC microservice (Deployment + Service + HPA) kdp/worker Background job worker (Deployment, no ingress) kdp/cronjob Scheduled batch job kdp/postgres PostgreSQL with automated backups kdp/redis Redis cache with Sentinel

docs.title: Deploying the microservice Chart
docs.text:
helm install <release-name> kdp/microservice \
  --namespace <namespace> \
  --values values.yaml

docs.title: Helm Chart Deployment
docs.text:
graph TD

A[Developer Commit]
--> B[GitHub Repository]

B --> C[GitHub Actions]

C --> D[Docker Build]

D --> E[Push to Registry]

E --> F[Helm Upgrade]

F --> G[Kubernetes Deployment]

G --> H[ReplicaSet]

H --> I[Pods Running]

I --> J[Ingress Access]

docs.title: Upgrading a Release
docs.text:
helm upgrade <release-name> kdp/microservice \
  --namespace <namespace> \
  --values values.yaml \
  --set image.tag=1.3.0

docs.title: Listing and Inspecting Releases
docs.text:
helm list -n my-team-prod                   # list all releases in a namespace
helm status my-service -n my-team-prod      # status of a specific release
helm get values my-service -n my-team-prod  # view effective values

docs.title: Custom Charts
docs.text:
Teams can maintain their own charts alongside the platform charts. Store custom charts in your Git repository under a charts/ directory and reference them locally:

helm install my-custom-service ./charts/my-custom-service \
  --namespace my-team-dev \
  --values ./charts/my-custom-service/values-dev.yaml

docs.title: Chart Versioning
docs.text:
Always pin chart versions in CI to avoid unexpected changes from upstream updates:

helm install my-service kdp/microservice --version 2.4.1 ...

Run helm search repo kdp --versions to see all available chart versions.

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: CI/CD Pipelines
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The platform integrates with GitHub Actions and GitLab CI out of the box. The recommended pipeline pattern is:

build → test → push image → deploy to dev → promote to staging → promote to production

docs.title: GitHub Actions Integration
docs.text:
The platform provides reusable workflow actions published at kdp-actions/deploy. A typical pipeline:

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Build and Deploy

on:
  push:
    branches: 
      - main
  pull_request:
    branches: 
      - main

jobs:
  build-and-test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Build Docker image
        run: |
          docker build -t $IMAGE_NAME:$GITHUB_SHA .
          docker push $IMAGE_NAME:$GITHUB_SHA
        env:
          IMAGE_NAME: registry.internal.example.com/my-team/my-service

  deploy-dev:
    needs: build-and-test
    if: github.event_name == 'push'
    uses: kdp-actions/deploy/.github/workflows/helm-deploy.yml@v1
    with:
      cluster: dev-us-east-1
      namespace: my-team-dev
      release: my-service
      chart: kdp/microservice
      image_tag: "${{ github.sha }}"
    secrets:
      KUBECONFIG: "${{ secrets.KDP_DEV_KUBECONFIG }}"

  deploy-prod:
    needs: deploy-dev
    environment: production           # requires manual approval in GitHub
    uses: kdp-actions/deploy/.github/workflows/helm-deploy.yml@v1
    with:
      cluster: prod-us-east-1
      namespace: my-team-prod
      release: my-service
      chart: kdp/microservice
      image_tag: "${{ github.sha }}"
    secrets:
      KUBECONFIG: "${{ secrets.KDP_PROD_KUBECONFIG }}"

docs.title: GitLab CI Integration
docs.text:
# .gitlab-ci.yml
stages:
  - build
  - deploy-dev
  - deploy-prod

build:
  stage: build
  script:
    - docker build -t $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHA .
    - docker push $CI_REGISTRY_IMAGE:$CI_COMMIT_SHA

deploy-dev:
  stage: deploy-dev
  script:
    - kdp-cli deploy --cluster dev-us-east-1 --namespace my-team-dev
        --release my-service --image-tag $CI_COMMIT_SHA
  only:
    - main

deploy-prod:
  stage: deploy-prod
  script:
    - kdp-cli deploy --cluster prod-us-east-1 --namespace my-team-prod
        --release my-service --image-tag $CI_COMMIT_SHA
  when: manual
  only:
    - main

docs.title: Pipeline Best Practices
docs.text:
• Never deploy untagged images to production. Use the Git SHA or a semantic version tag.
  • Gate production deployments behind a manual approval step or a change-management ticket reference.
  • Run helm diff (using the helm-diff plugin) before every upgrade to preview changes before they apply.
  • Store kubeconfig credentials as encrypted CI secrets, never in source code.
  • Keep dev and prod pipelines symmetric — what runs in dev is exactly what runs in prod, only the cluster and values file differ.

docs.title: Viewing Deployment History
docs.text:
helm history my-service -n my-team-prod

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: Autoscaling
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The platform supports two autoscaling mechanisms:

  • Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) — scales the number of pod replicas
  • Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) — right-sizes resource requests per pod

Cluster-level node autoscaling is managed by the platform team and is enabled by default on staging and production clusters.

docs.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)
docs.text: HPA scales the number of pod replicas based on CPU utilisation, memory utilisation, or custom metrics.

docs.title: Enable via Helm Chart
docs.text:
# values.yaml
autoscaling:
  enabled: true
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70
  targetMemoryUtilizationPercentage: 80

docs.title: Apply Directly with kubectl
docs.text:
kubectl autoscale deployment my-service \
  --namespace my-team-prod \
  --min=2 --max=10 \
  --cpu-percent=70

docs.title: Check HPA Status
docs.text:
kubectl get hpa -n my-team-prod
kubectl describe hpa my-service -n my-team-prod

docs.title: Custom Metrics Autoscaling (KEDA)
docs.text:
For scaling on application-specific metrics (e.g., queue depth, request latency), the platform integrates with KEDA (Kubernetes Event-driven Autoscaling).

apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: my-service-scaledobject
  namespace: my-team-prod
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: my-service
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  triggers:
    - type: prometheus
      metadata:
        serverAddress: http://prometheus.monitoring.svc:9090
        metricName: http_requests_in_flight
        threshold: "50"
        query: sum(http_requests_in_flight{service="my-service"})

docs.title: Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)
docs.text:
VPA automatically adjusts resource requests and limits based on observed usage. It is recommended for right-sizing during initial rollout.

apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: my-service-vpa
  namespace: my-team-prod
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: my-service
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "Off"   # "Off" = recommendations only; "Auto" = live adjustment

Warning: Do not run HPA (on CPU/memory) and VPA in Auto mode simultaneously on the same deployment — they will conflict. Use HPA for replica count and VPA in Off mode for recommendations only.

docs.title: Autoscaling Tips
docs.text:
• Always set minReplicas: 2 in production to ensure high availability during scale-down events.
  • Set conservative maxReplicas limits to protect downstream dependencies and avoid runaway cost.
  • Tune HPA cooldown periods with --horizontal-pod-autoscaler-downscale-stabilization if you see flapping.
  • Check HPA events for scaling activity: kubectl describe hpa my-service -n my-team-prod

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The platform ships with a pre-configured observability stack:

Tool Purpose Internal URL Prometheus Metrics collection & alerting https://prometheus.<cluster>.internal.example.com Grafana Dashboards & visualisation https://grafana.<cluster>.internal.example.com Loki Log aggregation (via Grafana) Available as a data source within Grafana

docs.title: Exposing Metrics from Your Service
docs.text: The platform expects services to expose Prometheus metrics on /metrics at the service port.

docs.title: Enable via Helm Chart
docs.text:
# values.yaml — kdp/microservice chart
metrics:
  enabled: true
    port: 8080
    path: /metrics

docs.title: Annotate Your Pod Spec Directly
docs.text:
metadata:
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "8080"
    prometheus.io/path: "/metrics"

docs.title: Pre-built Grafana Dashboards
docs.text:
The platform team maintains a dashboard library. Import from the Grafana UI under Dashboards → Browse → KDP Library:

Dashboard Description KDP: Service Overview Request rate, error rate, latency (RED metrics) KDP: JVM / Node.js Runtime Heap, GC, event loop metrics KDP: Pod Resources CPU/memory usage vs. limits KDP: HPA Status Replica counts and scaling events

docs.title: Alerting
docs.text:
Alerts are defined as PrometheusRule resources. Add custom alerts for your service:

apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: my-service-alerts
  namespace: my-team-prod
  labels:
    release: kube-prometheus-stack   # required for Prometheus to pick up the rule
spec:
  groups:
    - name: my-service
      rules:
        - alert: HighErrorRate
          expr: |
            rate(http_requests_total{service="my-service", status=~"5.."}[5m])
            /
            rate(http_requests_total{service="my-service"}[5m]) > 0.05
          for: 2m
          labels:
            severity: warning
            team: my-team
          annotations:
            summary: "High error rate on my-service"
            description: "Error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }} over the last 5 minutes."

Alerts route to PagerDuty, Slack, or email based on the team label. Configure your routing in the platform portal under Alerting → Notification Policies.

docs.title: Query via Grafana (Loki)
docs.text:
Go to Grafana → Explore → Loki and use LogQL:

{namespace="my-team-prod", app="my-service"} |= "ERROR"

docs.title: Stream Logs via kubectl
docs.text:
# Stream logs from a deployment
kubectl logs -n my-team-prod deploy/my-service -f --tail=100

# Stream logs from all pods matching a label
kubectl logs -n my-team-prod -l app=my-service --all-containers=true

docs.title: Default Platform Alerts
docs.text:
The platform includes built-in alerts for common failure conditions:

  • Pod crash-looping (CrashLoopBackOff)
  • High pod restart count
  • Node memory pressure
  • Node disk pressure
  • Persistent volume claim unbound

These fire into the #platform-alerts Slack channel by default.

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: Rollbacks
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Every Helm upgrade is recorded in the cluster as a versioned release revision. If a deployment introduces a regression, you can roll back to any prior revision in seconds.

docs.title: Viewing Release History
docs.text:
helm history my-service -n my-team-prod

Example output:

REVISION  UPDATED                   STATUS     CHART               APP VERSION  DESCRIPTION
1         2026-05-01 09:12:00 UTC   superseded microservice-2.4.1  1.0.0        Install complete
2         2026-05-15 14:30:00 UTC   superseded microservice-2.4.1  1.1.0        Upgrade complete
3         2026-05-26 11:00:00 UTC   deployed   microservice-2.4.1  1.2.0        Upgrade complete

docs.title: Rolling Back with Helm
docs.text:
# Roll back to the immediately previous revision
helm rollback my-service -n my-team-prod

# Roll back to a specific revision number
helm rollback my-service 2 -n my-team-prod

Helm re-applies the exact Kubernetes manifests from the target revision. The rollback itself is recorded as a new revision entry.

docs.title: Rolling Back with kubectl
docs.text:
If you deployed directly with kubectl rather than Helm:

# Undo the most recent rollout
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-service -n my-team-prod

# Undo to a specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/my-service -n my-team-prod --to-revision=3

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/my-service -n my-team-prod

docs.title: Monitoring a Rollback
docs.text:
Watch pod replacement in real time:

kubectl rollout status deployment/my-service -n my-team-prod -w

docs.title: Automated Rollback in CI/CD
docs.text:
The platform's reusable GitHub Action supports automatic rollback on health-check failure:

with:
  rollback_on_failure: true
  health_check_url: https://my-service.internal.example.com/health
  health_check_timeout: 120   # seconds to wait before declaring failure

docs.title: Atomic Upgrades
docs.text:
Always use --atomic during automated Helm upgrades. This automatically rolls back if any resource fails to become ready within the timeout:

helm upgrade my-service kdp/microservice \
  --atomic \
  --timeout 5m \
  --namespace my-team-prod \
  --values values.yaml

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Tag every image with an immutable identifier (Git SHA or semantic version) so rollbacks retrieve the exact same binary.
  • Test the rollback path in staging before every major release.
  • Retain at least 10 Helm revisions (the default) to give yourself room to roll back across several releases.
  • Use --atomic in CI to prevent partially broken releases from sitting in a degraded state.
  • Set a health check endpoint (/health or /readiness) in your service so rollback automation has something concrete to test against.

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.

docs.title: Secrets Management
docs.text: Prototype Notice: This platform is currently in prototype status. Features and APIs may change between releases.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Secrets are managed through HashiCorp Vault (for storage and rotation) and synced into Kubernetes using the External Secrets Operator (ESO). Do not store credentials, API keys, or certificates in Helm values files, environment variables committed to Git, or ConfigMaps.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Vault (source of truth) ↓ External Secrets Operator syncs on schedule Kubernetes Secret (native) ↓ Referenced by pod spec Running Pod (env var or volume mount)

  1. A secret is stored in Vault at a well-known path.
  2. An ExternalSecret resource in your namespace declares which Vault path to fetch.
  3. The External Secrets Operator syncs the value into a native Kubernetes Secret.
  4. Your pod references the Kubernetes Secret — no application code changes required.

docs.title: Storing a Secret in Vault
docs.text:
Access Vault via the platform portal or CLI:

vault login -method=oidc   # authenticate with your SSO credentials

vault kv put secret/my-team/my-service/prod \
  DB_PASSWORD="s3cur3P@ssw0rd" \
  API_KEY="abc123xyz"

Vault paths follow the convention:

''' secret///

'''

docs.title: Creating an ExternalSecret
docs.text:
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: my-service-secrets
  namespace: my-team-prod
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h          # how often to re-sync from Vault
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend        # ClusterSecretStore provisioned by the platform
    kind: ClusterSecretStore
  target:
    name: my-service-secrets   # name of the resulting Kubernetes Secret
    creationPolicy: Owner
  data:
    - secretKey: DB_PASSWORD   # key in the Kubernetes Secret
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/my-team/my-service/prod
        property: DB_PASSWORD
    - secretKey: API_KEY
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/my-team/my-service/prod
        property: API_KEY

Apply and verify:

kubectl apply -f external-secret.yaml -n my-team-prod
kubectl get externalsecret my-service-secrets -n my-team-prod

docs.title: Inject All Keys as Environment Variables
docs.text:
spec:
  containers:
    - name: my-service
      envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: my-service-secrets

docs.title: Reference Individual Keys
docs.text:
env:
  - name: DB_PASSWORD
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: my-service-secrets
        key: DB_PASSWORD

docs.title: Secret Rotation
docs.text:
When a secret is updated in Vault, the External Secrets Operator re-syncs it on the next refresh cycle (default: 1 hour). To force an immediate sync:

kubectl annotate externalsecret my-service-secrets \
  -n my-team-prod \
  force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite

After rotation, restart the deployment to pick up new environment variable values:

kubectl rollout restart deployment/my-service -n my-team-prod

docs.title: TLS Certificates
docs.text:
TLS certificates are managed by cert-manager and provisioned automatically for any Ingress with the appropriate annotation:

# For internet-facing services
annotations:
  cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: "letsencrypt-prod"

# For internal services
annotations:
  cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: "internal-ca"

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Grant Vault policies with least privilege — each service should only have read access to its own secret path.
  • Never log secrets. Audit your application's startup logs and error handlers.
  • Rotate secrets on a schedule. The platform supports automated Vault lease renewal for database credentials.
  • Do not bake secrets into Docker images. Treat images as public artifacts.
  • Use separate Vault paths per environment (/dev, /staging, /prod) so a compromised dev credential cannot access production.
  • Audit Vault access logs periodically via the platform portal under Security → Vault Audit.

Documentation version: 0.1.0 — May 2026 Maintained by the Platform Engineering team.
